title: Emotions & Opinions
shortSummary: Express how you feel and share opinions politely, with the indirectness Korean conversation prefers.
culturalNote: Koreans often express emotions and opinions with a degree of indirectness that protects social harmony, a value rooted in 눈치 (nunchi), the skill of reading a room and sensing what others feel without being told. Strong disagreement is usually softened, so instead of a flat 'no' you might hear 좀 그래요 (jom geuraeyo, it's a bit much) or a gentle hesitation. Saying 글쎄요 (geulsseyo, well, I'm not sure) buys time and avoids bluntness. Positive feelings are shared more freely, and 기뻐요 (gippeoyo, I'm happy) or 재미있어요 (jaemiisseoyo, it's fun) come up often. When giving opinions, the phrase 제 생각에는 (je saenggageneun, in my opinion) frames a view modestly. Because tone and face matter so much, learners sound more natural and considerate when they hedge opinions and lead with empathy rather than stating conclusions too directly.

grammar.explanation: To connect a cause and a feeling, attach 아서 or 어서 to a verb or adjective stem, meaning 'because / so'. 기쁘다 (gippeuda, happy) plus a reason: 시험에 붙어서 기뻐요 (because I passed the exam, I'm happy).

examples.en: I'm happy because I met a friend.

grammar.commonMistake: Adding a tense to the first verb; -아서/어서 stays in plain form and the tense goes on the final verb.

grammar.explanation: To state an opinion gently, end with 것 같아요 (geot gatayo, it seems / I think). It makes a claim less direct, which Korean conversation prefers. 좋다 (jota, good) becomes 좋은 것 같아요 (it seems good).

examples.native: 이게 더 좋은 것 같아요. (ige deo joeun geot gatayo.)
examples.en: I think this one is better.

grammar.commonMistake: Stating opinions too bluntly without 것 같아요, which can sound abrupt or overbearing in Korean.

dialogue.setup: Two colleagues, Hyun and Olivia, discuss a new project plan.

lines.speaker: 현
lines.english: Olivia, what do you think of the new plan?

lines.speaker: 올리비아
lines.native: 음, 제 생각에는 조금 어려운 것 같아요.
lines.transliteration: eum, je saenggageneun jogeum eoryeoun geot gatayo.
lines.english: Hmm, in my opinion it seems a bit difficult.

lines.speaker: 현
lines.english: That's right. I'm worried because there isn't enough time.

lines.speaker: 올리비아
lines.native: 그래도 같이 하면 될 것 같아요.
lines.transliteration: geuraedo gachi hamyeon doel geot gatayo.
lines.english: Still, I think we can do it if we work together.

quiz.question: How do you say 'In my opinion' in Korean?
quiz.explanation: 제 생각에는 (je saenggageneun) frames an opinion modestly, meaning 'in my opinion'.

quiz.question: Which phrase softly signals 'I'm not sure' to avoid a blunt answer?
quiz.explanation: 글쎄요 (geulsseyo) hedges politely, buying time instead of saying a flat yes or no.

quiz.question: 이게 더 좋은 것 _____. (it seems / I think)
quiz.explanation: 것 같아요 (geot gatayo) softens an opinion, so 좋은 것 같아요 means 'I think it's good'.

quiz.question: What does the cultural concept 눈치 (nunchi) describe?
options: Reading a room and sensing others' feelings
quiz.correctAnswer: Reading a room and sensing others' feelings
quiz.explanation: 눈치 (nunchi) is the social skill of sensing what others feel without being told.

quiz.question: Arrange: 기뻐요 / 만나서 / 친구를 (I'm happy because I met a friend)
quiz.explanation: Object, then reason with -아서/어서, then the feeling: 친구를 만나서 기뻐요.
